items.q: Which method of heat transfer cooks food under a grill?
items.correct: Radiation, as infrared heat reaches the food directly.
items.misconception: Conduction through direct contact with the grill.
items.random_wrong: Evaporation of water from the food.
items.why: Students assume contact (conduction) when grilling actually relies on radiated heat.

items.q: Is boiling vegetables for a long time the best way to keep their vitamins?
items.correct: No; prolonged boiling leaches out water-soluble vitamins, so steaming preserves more.
items.misconception: Longer boiling makes vegetables more nutritious.
items.random_wrong: Boiling adds vitamins to the water.
items.why: Learners think more cooking means more goodness, missing vitamin loss into the water.

items.q: Why are the 'bridge' and 'claw' grips taught for using knives?
items.correct: To cut safely and control the food, reducing the risk of cutting fingers.
items.misconception: To make food look more decorative.
items.why: Students see knife grips as presentation techniques rather than safety methods.

items.q: Heat reaching food directly by infrared waves under a grill is transferred by:
items.why: It's Radiation. “Conduction” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Boiling and steaming are examples of:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Moist cooking methods. “Dry cooking methods” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: The 'claw' and 'bridge' grips are used to ensure safe:
items.why: It's Knife skills. “Oven use” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Heat transferred through direct contact from a hot frying pan to food is by:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Conduction. “Radiation” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
